landing.metaTitle: Atelier — The freelance marketplace that pays providers in full
landing.metaDescription: Zero-commission B2B marketplace: 0% on the client side, capped flat fees on the freelance side, agencies and referrers rated on real missions. Secure payments via Stripe Connect.
landing.metaOgAlt: Atelier · The freelance marketplace with 0% commission

hero.subtitleDesktop: Atelier is the first freelance marketplace that also lists agencies and business referrers — and rates them all by the clients they have actually served.
hero.subtitleMobile: The freelance marketplace that also lists agencies and business referrers — rated by the clients they have actually served.

search.queryPlaceholderAgency: Branding studio, dev agency, design collective...

features.card1Title: You pay. Your freelance receives. No middleman.
features.card1Body: No commission taken. Funds are held in escrow via Stripe Connect for the duration of the mission, then released in full to the provider on validation.
features.card2Title: The first marketplace that also rates agencies.
features.card2Body: Until now you had to take an agency's word for the reviews on its own site. Atelier rates them like freelancers: public, verified reviews tied to real missions.
features.card3Title: For the first time, referrers are rated.
features.card3Body: Their average score reflects the freelancers they have recommended. The opaque network is over: the real quality of their introductions is public.

pricing.intro: We bring exactly the same value as other marketplaces, without taking a cut. Here is how.
pricing.step1Body: When you hire a provider, the mission amount is held by Stripe Connect — not by us, by Stripe. The provider knows they will get paid. You know payment only happens once you validate.
pricing.step2Body: During the mission, you can report a problem in one click. Our team arbitrates. Funds stay in escrow until the situation is resolved.
pricing.step3Body: Once the mission is validated, funds are released to the provider in full. No percentage. No hidden retention.
pricing.rowReferrerDetail: No fee. You receive your negotiated commission in full.
pricing.rowFreelanceDetail: Flat fee on missions of €1,000 and above. Capped. €0 with subscription.
pricing.rowAgencyDetail: Flat fee on missions of €2,500 and above. Capped. €0 with subscription.
pricing.footnote: Bottom line: neither the client nor the referrer pays anything. The provider's flat fees disappear with an Atelier subscription.

credits.p1: You can post listings and you can apply to other people's. Applying takes credits.
credits.p2Trail: And that's it: no purchases possible. We won't run a pay-to-win system where the highest bidder jumps the line.
credits.p3Accent: every signed mission earns you credits:
credits.p3Trail: we consider your profile more relevant when you deliver. Agencies that want to apply a lot just need to chain missions — it's coherent and it shields us from spam.
credits.cardFootnote: No purchases. Credits come only from the weekly reset and missions you deliver.

referrers.titleTrail: rated on the quality of what they bring.
referrers.p1: Before Atelier, business referral was a network game. The most visible captured the best missions, with no client able to tell whether their recommendations held up. Quiet skilled people stayed invisible; well-connected talkers grabbed everything.
referrers.p2Highlight: a referrer's rating is the average of the ratings of every freelancer they have recommended on completed missions.
referrers.p2Suffix: Not their charisma. Not their address book. The work delivered.
referrers.cardFootnote: Camille's score = average of the 14 ratings above, weighted by mission volume. Updated on every closure.

agencies.p1: Until now, picking an agency meant reading the testimonials they post on their own site. Three glowing quotes from no-one-knows-who, and you sign for tens of thousands of euros.
agencies.p2Highlight: On Atelier, agencies and freelancers go through the same filter.
agencies.p2Trail: Public, verified reviews tied to real missions. The studios that genuinely deliver come out on top. The rest do too.
agencies.compareBeforeFootnote: Reviews on their own site. Verifiable? No idea.
agencies.compareAfterFootnote: Reviews tied to real missions, signed by clients.

subprocessors.title: Sub-processors
subprocessors.intro: We rely on the sub-processors listed below to operate the service. The list is kept up to date; for any question, write to dpo@designedtrust.com. For each non-EU sub-processor, the transfer legal basis is specified (SCC = Standard Contractual Clauses 2021/914 — primary basis; DPF = EU-US Data Privacy Framework 2023/1795 — supplementary only). A Transfer Impact Assessment (TIA) is documented internally for each transfer.
subprocessors.dpfNote: Important — Schrems II: the DPF is mobilised as a supplementary mechanism and is never the sole legal basis of a transfer. The primary basis remains the Standard Contractual Clauses 2021/914 + supplementary measures (TLS + at-rest encryption, AI training opt-out, minimisation, identifier anonymisation).
subprocessors.auditNote: The DPO reviews the DPF registry quarterly (https://www.dataprivacyframework.gov/list) to confirm the active self-certification status of each US sub-processor. Removal of a sub-processor from the DPF triggers automatic fallback to SCC + a TIA re-evaluation.

automatedDecisions.title: Automated decisions and right to human review
automatedDecisions.intro: Pursuant to article 22 of the GDPR, we disclose the automated decisions that may affect your experience on the marketplace. You have the right to obtain human intervention and to contest each of these decisions.

systems.moderationDescription: Our providers OpenAI (omni-moderation) and AWS Rekognition analyze the texts, images, audios and videos posted on the platform to detect prohibited content (hate, harassment, violence, non-consensual sexual content, etc.). Above a configured confidence threshold, the content is automatically rejected.
systems.moderationConsequence: Consequence: your message, media or portfolio piece may be blocked or removed from the platform. You can request human review by filing the form below within 30 days.
systems.rankingDescription: Our search engine (Typesense) orders the displayed profiles, missions and offers using a deterministic combination of textual relevance, profile quality, activity freshness and engagement signals. No opaque machine learning is used — the weights are documented in our internal documentation.
systems.rankingConsequence: Consequence: your ranking in results can vary without manual intervention. If you observe a sudden visibility drop or a ranking error, you can request human review.
systems.paymentDescription: Stripe scores every payment against anti-fraud signals (Stripe Radar). Above a risk threshold, the transaction is declined by Stripe before reaching our systems.
systems.paymentConsequence: Consequence: a payment may be blocked without us being able to technically override the decision on the platform side — but we support affected users in their dialogue with Stripe and provide the contextual elements required.

automatedDecisions.rightsDescription: For each of these decisions, you have the right (1) to obtain an explanation of the underlying logic, (2) to express your point of view, (3) to obtain the intervention of a human member of our team, and (4) to contest the decision.
automatedDecisions.appealDescription: To exercise your right, write to {email} specifying the type of decision (moderation, ranking or payment), a reference (rejected message ID, transaction ID, etc.) and the reason for your request. We commit to respond within 30 days as required by GDPR art. 12-3.
automatedDecisions.appealAlternativeDescription: If our response does not satisfy you, you can refer the matter to the CNIL: {cnilUrl}.

aipd.title: Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)
aipd.subtitle: Document maintained under GDPR art. 35 for processing activities presenting high risk. Three distinct DPIAs are conducted.
aipd.scopeBody: Three activities have been identified as requiring a DPIA: biometric identity verification, automated moderation by third-party AI models, and commercial profiling for matching providers and clients.
aipd.methodBody: The analysis follows the CNIL method (PIA guide 1/2/3): description, evaluation of necessity and proportionality, identification of risks, planned measures, DPO opinion, controller decision.
aipd.aipd1Body: Activity framed by AML obligations (French Monetary and Financial Code art. L.561-2). Main risks: video frame interception in transit, unauthorised access to ID documents, false positives/negatives, algorithmic bias. Measures retained: TLS + at-rest encryption, no frame storage, mandatory human review at marginal scores, quarterly bias statistics.
aipd.aipd2Heading: DPIA-02 — Automated moderation by third-party AI models (OpenAI + Anthropic)
aipd.aipd2Body: Activity framed by the DSA and the LCEN. Main risks: false positives, bias, leakage via sub-processor logs, reuse for training. Measures retained: identifier anonymisation, contractual opt-out training clause, double moderation on media, right to human review within 30 days.
aipd.aipd3Heading: DPIA-03 — Commercial profiling and provider/client matching
aipd.aipd3Body: Deterministic Typesense ranking — no opaque machine learning. Main risks: economic side effects, structural bias, drift after update. Measures retained: public transparency of weights, visibility toggle, immediate de-indexing on opt-out.
aipd.residualBody: After measures, none of the three activities presents a high residual risk. Prior consultation with the CNIL (GDPR art. 36) is not required. An annual review is planned.
aipd.dpoBody: The DPO's reasoned opinion and the controller's motivated decision are recorded in the canonical Markdown. Signatures remain to be completed by the authorised individuals.

dpaTemplate.transferBody: The template references the EU-US DPF (EU 2023/1795 of 10 July 2023) for self-certified US sub-processors and the standard contractual clauses 2021/914 (Module 2) for other cases. A Transfer Impact Assessment (TIA) is required where supplementary measures are needed.
dpaTemplate.appendicesBody: Appendix I — Standard contractual clauses (SCC 2021/914 — Module 2). Appendix II — List of authorised sub-sub-processors. Appendix III — Sub-processor-specific technical and organisational measures.
dpaTemplate.signatureBody: The template leaves the signature of both Parties (Client + Sub-processor) pending with date and role. The [À COMPLÉTER] markers correspond to fields to be filled in on a per-contract basis.

politiqueConfidentialite.treatmentsIntro: The Platform operates eleven (11) processing activities documented in the GDPR art. 30 record. For each activity, the purpose, legal basis, data categories, recipients, non-EU transfers, retention duration, and security measures are specified. Synthetic summary:
politiqueConfidentialite.treatmentsItems: Account creation — Purpose: contract performance. Basis: Art. 6(1)(b). Data: email, bcrypt-hashed password, role, name/company name, connection IP. Recipients: Platform, Resend (email). Retention: account active + 30 days.|Public profile — Purpose: marketplace visibility. Basis: Art. 6(1)(b). Data: name, expertise, biography, city, languages, photo. Recipients: any visitor (public fields only). Retention: while active.|1-1 messaging — Purpose: enable User communication. Basis: Art. 6(1)(b). Data: content, metadata, media. Recipients: Platform, OpenAI (moderation). Retention: 3 years.|Payment / invoicing — Purpose: performance + accounting obligation. Basis: Art. 6(1)(b) + Art. 6(1)(c). Data: IBAN, amounts, invoices, mandate 289 I-2 CGI. Recipients: Stripe Payments Europe Ltd, DGFiP (DAC7). Retention: 10 years (French Commercial Code L.123-22).|Biometric KYC — Purpose: AML compliance. Basis: Art. 6(1)(c) + Art. 9(2)(a) explicit consent. Data: ID document, selfie, ephemeral liveness video. Recipients: Stripe Payments Europe Ltd, AWS Rekognition (EU region eu-west-1). Retention: 5 years after end of relationship (CMF L.561-12).|Search and matching — Purpose: deterministic recommendation. Basis: Art. 6(1)(b) + Art. 6(1)(f). Data: search criteria, aggregated engagement signals. Recipients: Platform, Typesense (EU). Retention: 12 months then anonymisation.|Automated moderation — Purpose: platform safety, DSA. Basis: Art. 6(1)(c) DSA + Art. 6(1)(f) legitimate interest. Data: text and media content. Recipients: OpenAI, AWS Rekognition. Retention: 90 days for moderation logs.|Push / email notifications — Purpose: inform the User. Basis: Art. 6(1)(b) (transactional) + Art. 6(1)(a) (marketing opt-in). Data: device tokens, preferences. Recipients: Firebase Cloud Messaging, Resend. Retention: 60 inactive days (tokens) / opt-out (marketing).|Audience measurement — Purpose: product improvement. Basis: Art. 6(1)(a) consent (opt-in via CMP). Data: anonymised events. Recipients: PostHog (Ireland), GA4 (US, DPF). Retention: 12 months.|Security audit log — Purpose: incident detection. Basis: Art. 6(1)(f) + Art. 6(1)(c). Data: append-only events without PII (post-sanitization). Recipients: Platform. Retention: 24 months hot + 24 months cold R2 archive.|Moderation and reports — Purpose: handling DSA art. 16 reports. Basis: Art. 6(1)(c) DSA + Art. 6(1)(f) safety. Data: report, resource ID, reason, reporter identity. Recipients: Trust & Safety team, authorities upon legal request. Retention: 3 years after case closure.
politiqueConfidentialite.retentionBody: Accounts: while active + 30 days. Messages: 3 years. Notifications: 90 days. Push tokens: 60 inactive days. Searches: 12 months then anonymisation. Revoked sessions: 30 days. Audit log: 24 months hot + 24 months cold R2 archive. Moderation reports: 3 years after closure. Payment and invoices: 10 years (French Commercial Code L.123-22). KYC: 5 years after end of relationship (CMF L.561-12). DAC7 fiscal data: 5 years (CGI 1649 ter §3). Beyond, data is deleted definitively or archived in irreversibly anonymised form.
politiqueConfidentialite.profilingBody: Three automated processes may affect your experience: (i) automated content moderation (OpenAI omni-moderation, AWS Rekognition, multi-model unanimity score ≥ 0.85 → temporary masking and human review within 24h); (ii) search ranking (deterministic Typesense, weights documented in internal documentation, no opaque learning); (iii) Stripe Radar payment risk scoring (Stripe proprietary model, possible refusal of high-risk transactions). For each, you have the right to obtain human intervention, express your view, and contest the decision (see /decisions-automatisees for the appeal procedure).
politiqueConfidentialite.transferBody: Applicable frameworks: (i) Standard contractual clauses EU decision 2021/914 of 4 June 2021 — primary legal basis; (ii) EU-US Data Privacy Framework EU decision 2023/1795 of 10 July 2023 — mobilised only as a supplementary mechanism, never as a sole basis given the post-Schrems II fragility of the framework. Supplementary measures: TLS 1.2+ encryption, at-rest encryption, contractual AI training opt-out (OpenAI Enterprise + Anthropic API), data minimisation, identifier anonymisation. A Transfer Impact Assessment (TIA) is documented internally for each non-EU sub-processor, and the US CLOUD Act scenario is explicitly evaluated.
politiqueConfidentialite.subprocessorsBody: The up-to-date public list is available at /sous-processeurs with, for each, country, purpose, and transfer mechanism (DPF / SCC). Material sub-processor changes are announced on this page with reasonable notice. To object to a new sub-processor, write to dpo@designedtrust.com — the consequence may be Account termination if objection makes the service technically impossible.
politiqueConfidentialite.specialCategoriesBody: Biometric data collected under KYC (liveness video, facial comparison) is Art. 9(1) GDPR data. It is processed on the basis of your explicit consent (Art. 9(2)(a)) collected before upload, supplementing the AML legal obligation (Art. 6(1)(c)). No video frame is retained — only the binary match score is logged. You can withdraw this consent at any time, with the understanding that you will then no longer be able to receive payments via the Platform.
politiqueConfidentialite.minorsBody: The Platform is strictly reserved for adult individuals acting in a professional capacity. No data of a child is knowingly collected. If you observe that a minor has created an account, report it to support@designedtrust.com — we will proceed to deletion within 5 business days.
politiqueConfidentialite.breachBody: In case of a personal data breach likely to result in a risk to your rights and freedoms, the Platform notifies the CNIL within 72 hours (GDPR art. 33) and, in case of high risk, informs you directly by email and in-app banner (art. 34). The breach register is maintained in accordance with art. 33(5).
politiqueConfidentialite.automatedDecisionsBody: See /decisions-automatisees for the details of the three automated systems (moderation, ranking, payment) and the human-review request procedure.
politiqueConfidentialite.contactBody: For any question, write to dpo@designedtrust.com — reply within 30 days (GDPR art. 12-3). If our reply does not satisfy you, you may refer the matter to the CNIL: cnil.fr/fr/plaintes — 3 place de Fontenoy, TSA 80715, 75334 PARIS CEDEX 07.

cgu.title: Terms of use (CGU)
cgu.subtitle: Terms governing the use of the Platform by agencies, freelancers, enterprises and business referrers. Service strictly reserved for professional use (B2B). In force from the version date above.
cgu.definitionsIntro: The terms used in these CGU have the following meaning:
cgu.definitionsItems: "Platform" means the online service accessible at services.designedtrust.com, published by Designed Trust SAS.|"User" means any adult individual acting strictly in a professional capacity who creates an Account on the Platform.|"Client" means the User (or Organisation they represent) posting a Mission and paying for the service.|"Provider" means the User executing the Mission, whether an Agency (legal entity) or a Freelance (independent individual).|"Business referrer" means a Freelance User who has enabled the referrer_enabled toggle and introduces a Client to a Provider in exchange for a commission agreed tripartitely.|"Organisation" means the legal entity (company, registered freelancer, association) on whose behalf the User acts. The Organisation owns subscriptions, the wallet, invoices and the Stripe Connect account.|"Mission" means the service described by the Client and executed by the Provider.|"Proposal" means the Provider's commercial offer in response to a Mission.|"Milestone" means a payment step of a Mission, triggering a partial payout to the Provider after Client validation.|"Code of conduct" means the document published at /legal/code-de-conduite, an integral part of these CGU.
cgu.objectBody: These CGU define the conditions under which the Platform makes its services available to Users. Acceptance is materialised by Account creation or, for unregistered visitors, by mere browsing of the public pages. Continued use constitutes renewed acceptance.
cgu.accessBody: Access to the Platform is reserved for adult individuals acting in a professional capacity. One Account per person. KYC identity verification (see Sales Terms art. 2) is required to receive payments. The User is solely responsible for the confidentiality of their credentials and for any action taken from their Account. In case of suspected compromise, the User must notify support@designedtrust.com without delay.
cgu.behaviorBody: The User undertakes to comply with the Code of conduct published at /legal/code-de-conduite, an integral part of these CGU. Prohibited content includes: harassment, discrimination, insults, hate speech, non-consensual sexual content, violence, medical misinformation, doxxing, fake accounts, fake reviews, platform circumvention, fraud, counterfeiting, third-party intellectual property infringement, and content falling under Stripe Restricted Businesses (weapons, drugs, unregulated financial services, gambling, cryptocurrencies, etc.).
cgu.behaviorCodeOfConduct: The detailed Code of conduct at /legal/code-de-conduite lists prohibited behaviours and graduated sanctions (warning, suspension 7/30/90 days, permanent ban), together with the reporting and appeal procedure.
cgu.financeBody: Every financial flow stemming from a Platform-initiated relationship must transit through the Platform. The commission follows the schedule set in article 1 of the Sales Terms: 5 % HT on the Client side + 10 % HT on the Provider side + where applicable 5 % HT business-referral commission (deducted from the Platform's Provider-side commission). Stripe processing fees are passed through at cost exclusively to the Client.
cgu.antiCircumventionBody: From the initial introduction on the Platform and for twelve (12) months after the end of any Mission or Proposal, the User undertakes not to:
cgu.antiCircumventionItems: Share direct personal contact details (email, phone, postal address) with another User before the formal acceptance of a Proposal;|Bypass the Platform's payment mechanism to invoice directly a service following an introduction initiated on the Platform;|Solicit, hire or contract directly with another User met through the Platform, without notifying the Platform and paying the commissions due;|Encourage another User to leave the Platform to pursue the relationship off-platform.
cgu.antiCircumventionSanction: Breach of this clause triggers: (i) immediate Account suspension; (ii) liquidated damages equal to six (6) months of estimated commissions on the bypassed service (article 1231-5 French Civil Code), without prejudice to any additional damage; (iii) where applicable, unfair-competition proceedings (article 1240 French Civil Code).
cgu.ipBody: The User retains ownership of their content but grants the Platform a non-exclusive, worldwide, royalty-free licence, for the time the content remains on the Platform, strictly limited to the execution of the Service (display, backup, indexation, moderation, transmission to Mission recipients). Platform components (logo, code, design system, structured database) remain the exclusive property of Designed Trust SAS.
cgu.liabilityBody: The Platform acts as a technical intermediary under article 6 of the French LCEN n° 2004-575 and the DSA Regulation (EU) 2022/2065. It is not a party to the contracts entered between Users and does not intervene in the negotiation, execution or termination of Missions. The Platform may not be qualified as employer, agent, commercial agent, broker or commercial representative of the Users. The Platform's total liability, on all grounds (contractual, tort, quasi-tort, indemnity), under these CGU is strictly limited to the cumulative amount of commissions effectively paid by the requesting User to the Platform during the twelve (12) months preceding the triggering event, and cannot exceed ten thousand euros (10,000 € HT) per claim and per calendar year. This limitation does not apply to damages from wilful or gross misconduct of the Platform, nor to bodily injury (articles 1170 and 1231-3 French Civil Code).
cgu.workerClassificationHeading: Article 9 — Independent status of the Provider
cgu.workerClassificationIntro: The Provider declares and warrants that they carry on their activity strictly independently, freely organising their work, with no subordination to the Platform or the Client. The Provider assumes full responsibility for:
cgu.workerClassificationItems: Their organisation, schedule and work tools;|Their tax and social compliance (URSSAF, taxes, VAT);|Their professional insurance (RC pro, contingency cover);|Their professional qualifications (degree, certification, RCS or trade-register registration).
cgu.workerClassificationDuration: 9.2 No exclusivity — No Client may impose an exclusivity clause restricting the Provider's right to work for other Clients or via other platforms. Any contrary clause in a Proposal or contractual exchange is deemed not written. 9.3 Maximum mission duration — A Mission cannot exceed twelve (12) consecutive months with the same Client without an explicit contractual review accepted by the Parties. Beyond twenty-four (24) consecutive months with the same Client, the Platform reserves the right to notify the Parties of a reclassification risk and to propose an ad hoc contract template.
cgu.workerClassificationPlurality: 9.4 Plurality of Clients — The Platform encourages the Provider to diversify their Clients to preserve economic independence. An economic dependence on a single Client greater than 70 % of the Provider's annual revenue is a risk indicator that the Parties are invited to correct.
cgu.workerClassificationDisclaimer: 9.5 Worker Classification — Pursuant to applicable case law (Cass. soc. Uber 4 March 2020, Deliveroo 13 April 2022, Take Eat Easy 28 Nov. 2018), the Client alone is responsible for the legal classification of the contractual relationship they enter with the Provider via the Platform. The Platform has neither the role nor the competence to classify the legal nature of that relationship and cannot be held liable for any reclassification as an employment contract. Nothing in these CGU, a Proposal or a Mission shall be construed as creating an employment, partnership, joint venture, franchise or mandate relationship between the Platform and the User.
cgu.referrerBody: A Freelance User may enable the referrer_enabled toggle to introduce a Client to a Provider and receive a referral commission. This activity is subject to: (i) tripartite express agreement (Client + Provider + Referrer) traced on the Platform; (ii) the Referrer being a registered professional (RCS, micro-entrepreneur or equivalent) with a valid NIF; (iii) AML/KYC compliance (French Monetary and Financial Code art. L.561-2); (iv) issuance of an invoice by the Referrer to the Platform for the commission received, under the invoicing mandate described in article 7 of the Sales Terms. Failure of any of these conditions defeats payment of the commission. The Referrer has no access to the 1-1 conversation between Client and Provider after the initial introduction, unless all three Parties expressly agree.
cgu.moderationBody: The Platform implements ex post content moderation, paired with preventive automated moderation (OpenAI omni-moderation, AWS Rekognition, and where applicable AWS Comprehend). Any User may report illegal content via the reporting button available on every profile, message or proposal. The Platform commits to:
cgu.moderationItems: Acknowledge receipt of the report within 48 hours;|Review the report within 10 business days;|Notify a motivated decision to the content author and the reporter (Notice of Limitation, DSA art. 17);|Offer an internal appeal procedure via support@designedtrust.com (response within 10 business days);|Preserve judicial remedies and the right to seise the Digital Services Coordinator (ARCOM in France).
cgu.suspensionHeading: Article 12 — Suspension and termination by the Platform
cgu.suspensionBody: The Platform reserves the right to suspend or terminate the Account of any User in breach of these CGU, the Code of conduct, or the law. Graduated sanctions: warning, temporary suspension (7, 30 or 90 days, read-only access, in-flight payments protected), permanent ban (Account closure, in-flight payments settled, Platform commission retained). In case of suspected fraud, funds may be immediately frozen under Stripe KYC review. Any significant suspension decision is motivated in writing and subject to appeal within 30 days at support@designedtrust.com.
cgu.terminationBody: The User can close their Account at any time via /dashboard/account/gdpr. Definitive deletion within 30 days, except for legal retention obligations (payment 10 years — French Commercial Code L.123-22; KYC 5 years — CMF L.561-12; audit log 24 months hot + 24 months cold). In-flight payments are settled per the cycle described in Sales Terms art. 3.
cgu.modificationBody: The Platform reserves the right to modify these CGU at any time. Any substantial modification is notified to the User by email and Platform publication with a minimum thirty (30) day notice. A User who does not accept the new CGU may terminate their Account free of charge during this notice; at expiry, continued use constitutes tacit acceptance (Pixmania case law 2018).
cgu.forceMajeureBody: Neither Party may be held liable for a failure resulting from a force majeure event as assessed by French case law (article 1218 French Civil Code), notably: natural disaster, armed conflict, large-scale cyber attack, public-authority decision, outage of a critical third-party sub-processor (Stripe, Vercel, Neon, Cloudflare). The occurrence of a force majeure event suspends the affected obligations. If the event lasts beyond sixty (60) days, either Party may terminate without indemnity.
cgu.lawBody: These CGU are governed by French law. As the Platform is strictly B2B, the Paris Commercial Court has jurisdiction, save for any mandatory contrary provision. The Parties first attempt amicable resolution; failing which, CMAP contractual mediation is open by mutual agreement. For the rare Users qualifying as consumers under the French Consumer Code (e.g. micro-entrepreneur acting outside their activity), consumer mediation remains available — reference being registered with the Médiateur National de la Consommation.

cgv.title: Sales terms (CGV)
cgv.subtitle: Terms governing commercial relationships and payments flowing through the Platform. Strictly B2B. Compliant with French Consumer Code L.111-1 (price transparency) and Commercial Code L.123-22 (10-year retention).
cgv.modelBody: Registration, browsing and profile publication on the Platform are free. The Platform earns its revenue from a service commission deducted from each accepted and paid Mission, set as follows:
cgv.modelItems: Client side: 5 % HT of the HT amount of each milestone;|Provider side: 10 % HT of the HT amount of each milestone (reduced to 5 % HT for Premium Providers);|Total Platform commission: 15 % HT per milestone (10 % HT for Premium Providers);|Where applicable, Business Referrer commission: 5 % HT of the milestone, deducted from the Provider-side Platform commission (not in addition), with tripartite express agreement traced on the Platform.
cgv.modelStripeFees: 1.2 Stripe fees — Stripe Payments Europe Ltd processing fees are passed through at cost, exclusively to the Client: 1.5 % + €0.25 per transaction for EEA-issued cards; 2.9 % + €0.25 per transaction for non-EEA cards; 0.8 % per transaction (capped at €5) for SEPA transfers. The applicable rate is set based on the payment method effectively chosen by the Client.
cgv.modelPremium: 1.3 Premium subscription — An optional Premium subscription is offered at the Organisation level, with features and price disclosed on the Premium page. Monthly billing with automatic renewal, cancellable at any time without fee with effect at the end of the current period. 1.4 Modification — Rates may be modified with a 30-day notice under article 14 of the CGU.
cgv.kycBody: Pursuant to AML obligations (French Monetary and Financial Code art. L.561-2 et seq.) and Stripe Connect Custom requirements, any Organisation wishing to receive payments must pass a prior identity verification operated by Stripe Payments Europe Ltd. This includes the collection of an official ID, an address proof, beneficial owners (UBO) where applicable, and a facial biometric verification by AWS Rekognition (EU region) — no video-frame storage. Target turnaround 5 business days. KYC data retained 5 years after end of contractual relationship (CMF L.561-12).
cgv.cycleBody: Mission posting by the Client → Provider Proposal → explicit acceptance by the Client (electronic signature) → full provisioning of funds with Stripe (escrow) → Mission execution → explicit or tacit validation (at expiry of the seven (7) day objection window from milestone delivery) → payout to the Provider within one (1) to three (3) business days via Stripe Connect → automated issuance of the invoice and receipt (article 7). The 7-day objection window is shorter than Malt or Upwork (14 days) — it favours quick payout to the Provider while preserving the Client's right to formalise a dispute.
cgv.paymentBody: Full provisioning at acceptance. Payment means: Stripe credit card (Visa, Mastercard, American Express) + SEPA transfer. Tacit validation occurs upon expiry of the 7-day objection window, except where the Client has formalised a dispute (written notice via /dashboard/disputes mentioning precise grounds and supporting documents). Upon validation, funds are released to the Provider per article 3. In the event of a chargeback after validation, the Provider undertakes to refund the corresponding amount on their wallet (Upwork section 9.3 type clause).
cgv.disputeBody: A 15-day prior amicable resolution attempt between the Parties is mandatory before opening a formal dispute. The formal dispute is opened via /dashboard/disputes; the Platform support team reviews it within 15 business days, with both Parties involved, without prejudice to the right to seise the Paris Commercial Court. After three (3) months from dispute opening without an arbitration or court decision, escrow funds are released to the Provider (Contra-style clause).
cgv.referrerBody: Business referral is enabled via the referrer_enabled toggle (see CGU art. 10). The default commission is 5 % HT of the HT amount of each milestone, deducted from the Platform commission on the Provider side (not in addition). Tripartite express agreement (Client + Provider + Referrer) is traced on the Platform before any payment. The Referrer must be a registered professional (RCS, micro-entrepreneur or equivalent), subject to KYC/AML, and holder of a Stripe Connected Account with a valid NIF. The Platform pays the commission to the Referrer through a documented Stripe Transfer Group for each transaction. Failure of any of these conditions defeats payment of the commission.
cgv.invoicesIntroBody: 7.1 Each validated milestone gives rise to the issuance of the following documents:
cgv.invoicesItems: An invoice from the Provider to the Client, for the HT amount + applicable VAT of the service;|An invoice from the Platform to the Provider, for the Provider-side Platform commission (10 % HT, or 5 % HT in Premium);|An invoice from the Platform to the Client, for the Client-side Platform commission (5 % HT) and pass-through Stripe fees;|Where applicable, an invoice from the Platform to the Provider on behalf of the Business Referrer (5 % HT), or directly from the Referrer to the Platform.
cgv.invoicesMandate: 7.2 Invoicing mandate — By accepting these CGV, the Provider expressly mandates the Platform to issue, in their name and on their behalf, the invoice referred to in 7.1 (a), under article 289 I-2 of the French General Tax Code. The mandate is revocable at any time with 30 days' notice via dpo@designedtrust.com. Any invoice issued by the Platform on behalf of the Provider expressly mentions "Invoice issued by Designed Trust SAS on behalf of [Provider's registered name]" and includes the mandatory legal mentions (article 242 nonies A of annex II to the CGI).
cgv.invoicesVat: 7.3 VAT — The Provider is solely liable for VAT charged to the Client. The Platform does not withhold, remit or declare VAT on behalf of the Provider. Where the Client is established in another EU Member State and is subject to VAT (valid intra-EU VAT number verified via VIES), the issued invoice bears the mention "Reverse charge — article 196 directive 2006/112/EC" and no French VAT is charged. The Platform commission follows the same regime (intra-EU B2B reverse charge).
cgv.invoicesRetention: 7.4 Retention — Invoices and receipts are retained on the Platform for ten (10) years from the issue date, in accordance with article L.123-22 of the French Commercial Code, and remain accessible to the User from their dashboard. The applicable statutory payment term is thirty (30) days under article L.441-1 of the Commercial Code, save for contrary agreement of the Parties.
cgv.dac7Body: Pursuant to Directive (EU) 2021/514 ("DAC7") transposed in article 1649 ter of the French CGI and the order of 27 December 2022, the Platform annually collects the following fiscal information for each Provider and Referrer exceeding €2,000 in revenue or 30 transactions during the calendar year: identity (name, address, date of birth), tax identification number (NIF) and intra-EU VAT number where applicable, quarterly gross revenue, commissions retained by the Platform, payment IBAN. This information is transmitted to the DGFiP via the impots.gouv.fr portal before 31 January of the following year. An individual summary is made available to the Provider in their dashboard before that date, under art. 1649 ter §2 CGI.
cgv.refundsBody: Before validation of a milestone, the Client may request a full refund at any time (Stripe escrow funds are returned within 5 business days). After explicit or tacit validation, no refund is possible save with the Provider's express agreement, or with the decision of the support team following a dispute (article 5). Platform commissions retained are definitively acquired upon milestone validation, save in case of proven Provider fraud (within the meaning of the French Criminal Code) or material breach noted by the Platform. Premium subscriptions are non-refundable; they remain active until the end of the paid period.
cgv.restrictedHeading: Article 10 — Prohibited services and content (Stripe Restricted Businesses alignment)
cgv.restrictedIntro: Pursuant to the Stripe Restricted Businesses policy and AML provisions, the following are strictly prohibited on the Platform:
cgv.restrictedItems: Unregulated financial services (informal money transfer, hawala, cryptocurrency mining/exchange, ICOs, monetary-function NFTs);|Gambling, sports betting, lotteries (save for ANJ-authorised operators with supporting evidence);|Weapons, ammunition, explosives, weapon accessories (under the French Internal Security Code);|Regulated substances (drugs, prescription medicines, medical devices without CE certification);|Sexual or pornographic services, escort services, adult content;|Counterfeits (clothing, accessories, cultural goods, pirated software);|Political, religious or activist services qualifying as paid solicitation;|Multi-Level Marketing, pyramid schemes, dropshipping of unverified products;|Services qualified as "high risk" by Stripe (third-party debt collection, subscription clairvoyance, third-party black-hat SEO optimisation services);|Any activity illegal under French law, EU law, or the law of the country of residence of the Client or Provider.
cgv.dormantHeading: Article 11 — Dormant accounts and unclaimed funds
cgv.dormantBody: An Account inactive for twenty-four (24) months is notified of pending closure; failing User response within 90 days, the Account is closed in accordance with GDPR procedures. Residual unclaimed funds beyond five (5) years (counted from the last identified operation) are handled in accordance with article L.312-19 of the French Monetary and Financial Code (holder notice + limitation periods). The Eckert law n° 2014-617 of 13 June 2014 does not directly apply to a platform wallet (which is not a bank account stricto sensu), but the Platform commits to an equivalent regime in terms of prior notification and traceability.
cgv.liabilityBody: The Platform's liability under these CGV is strictly limited as set out in article 8 of the CGU (12 months of commissions, cap of €10,000 HT per claim and per calendar year). Chargebacks, Stripe Radar fraud and any reverse payments are borne under the Stripe Connect Custom conditions signed by the Organisation during onboarding. The Platform does not guarantee the quality, conformity or solvency of Providers — the Platform is a technical intermediary under LCEN art. 6.
cgv.lawBody: These CGV are governed by French law. The Paris Commercial Court has jurisdiction in case of dispute, save for any mandatory contrary provision.

codeOfConduct.title: Code of conduct and moderation policy
codeOfConduct.subtitle: Atelier connects professionals, and trust between Users is the foundation of the marketplace. This Code of conduct defines expected behaviours, prohibited behaviours, applicable sanctions and the appeal procedure. It is an integral part of the CGU and binds every User of the Platform.
codeOfConduct.preambleBody: The Atelier marketplace (services.designedtrust.com) connects agencies, freelancers, enterprises and business referrers. The quality of the service rests on mutual respect, professional integrity and trust between Users. Every User — regardless of their role (Client, Provider, Referrer) — commits to comply with this Code of conduct, which is an integral part of the CGU.
codeOfConduct.prohibitedIntro: The following behaviours are strictly prohibited on the Platform and may trigger immediate sanctions:
codeOfConduct.prohibitedItems: Harassment — sexual, moral, racial, religious, or based on sexual orientation, gender identity, disability, nationality or any other discriminatory criterion;|Discrimination — refusal to provide service or to make a connection based on a discriminatory criterion under French Criminal Code art. 225-1 (origin, sex, orientation, disability, political views, religion, union, etc.);|Insults, abusive language, hate speech, threats of violence or damage to persons or property;|Doxxing — sharing another person's personal information without their consent (address, phone, private photos, etc.);|Fraud attempts — phishing, fake accounts, identity theft, fake KYC documents, transaction-history manipulation;|Off-platform circumvention — proposing or soliciting an off-platform relationship to avoid commissions, the #1 motive of Upwork bans and applied here on the same terms;|Fake reviews and rating-system manipulation — creation of fake accounts to self-rate or rate a partner, exchange of good ratings between Users, paid solicitation of reviews;|Unfair competition and disparagement — defamation, parasitism, abusive poaching (art. 1240 French Civil Code);|Plagiarism and work theft — appropriation of portfolios, proposals or deliverables of others;|Intellectual property infringement — unauthorised use of logos, trademarks, protected content;|Illegal, hateful, non-consensual sexual, violent content, serious medical misinformation;|Promotion of content falling under Stripe Restricted Businesses (see CGV art. 10) — crypto, weapons, drugs, unlicensed gambling, counterfeits, MLM, etc.;|Spam — mass unsolicited solicitation, large-scale copy-paste proposals, mass messaging, abuse of the notifications feature.
codeOfConduct.reportBody: Any User may report suspicious behaviour or content via the reporting button available on every profile, message, proposal and conversation. Reports are also accepted by email at trust@designedtrust.com, or at support@designedtrust.com in the absence of a dedicated trust mailbox. In accordance with DSA Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 art. 16, the Platform acknowledges receipt of the report within 48 hours and reviews it with priority commensurate to its severity. Good-faith reports expose their author to no sanction.
codeOfConduct.sanctionsIntro: Sanction severity is proportionate to the breach noted. The Platform applies the following grid, adjustable in light of case law and context:
codeOfConduct.sanctionsItems: Written warning (1st minor breach) — motivated notification + entry in the User's record. Payments and activity preserved;|Temporary suspension of 7, 30 or 90 days (serious breach or repeat offence) — read-only access, in-flight payments protected, new missions paused, written motivated notification;|Permanent ban (very serious breach or multi-recidivism) — definitive Account closure, in-flight payments settled per the CGV cycle, Platform commission retained, prohibition to create a new Account;|Immediate fund freeze (special fraud-suspicion case) — under Stripe Payments Europe Ltd KYC review; investigation within 30 days save for public-authority request extending the deadline.
codeOfConduct.appealBody: A sanctioned User may contest any significant moderation or suspension decision by email at support@designedtrust.com within thirty (30) days of notification. The Platform reviews the contestation within ten (10) business days and issues a motivated written decision. Judicial remedies (Paris Commercial Court — see CGU art. 16) remain fully preserved. The User may also refer the matter to the national Digital Services Coordinator (ARCOM in France) under DSA art. 21, and to the French Data Protection Authority (CNIL) in case of a significant automated decision (GDPR art. 22).
codeOfConduct.dsaBody: In accordance with DSA Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 articles 14 and 23, the Platform commits to: (i) maintain this Code of conduct in plain, simple, intelligible, user-friendly and unambiguous language, accessible in an indexable format; (ii) motivate in writing every significant moderation decision (Notice of Limitation, DSA art. 17) and offer an appeal procedure; (iii) apply proportionate measures against abuse (art. 23 — graduated suspension rather than immediate ban save for manifest severity).
codeOfConduct.transparencyBody: The Platform will publish an annual anonymised moderation report indicating: (i) the volume of reports received by category; (ii) the sanction rate (suspensions, bans) by type of breach; (iii) average processing times; (iv) the main appeal grounds and their success rate. The first report is expected in January 2027 for calendar year 2026.
codeOfConduct.reviewBody: The Code of conduct is reviewed at least annually and at every significant feature addition. Any substantial modification is notified to Users by email and Platform publication with a minimum thirty (30) day notice, in accordance with CGU art. 14. The version currently in force appears on this page with its version date.
